title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: This blood test can reveal whether your triglycerides fall into a healthy range. For most people, fasting triglycerides should be less than 150 mg per desoliter. 500 or higher puts you into the very high range. This means there is too much fat in your blood.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: Very high triglycerides can increase your risk of pancreatitis and other cardiovascular events like heart disease. Ask your doctor what your triglyceride level should be.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: In many cases, lifestyle changes can help maintain healthy triglyceride levels. These changes include eating a diet low in sweets, refined carbohydrates and alcohol, eating regular exercise, managing your weight and not smoking.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: Medications can also help treat impaired triglyceride levels. If your doctor prescribed medication to treat high triglycerides, it is important to take it as directed and report any side effects you may have.

title: ANH15145 LDL and HDL Cholesterol
text_en: If you or someone you know has been diagnosed with high cholesterol, it's important to understand what cholesterol is and why it's important to keep it under control. Cholesterol is a fat-like substance made by your liver and also comes from foods you intake that is then packaged into particles called lipoproteins. Your body needs cholesterol to make hormones, vitamin D, and a substance that helps you digest food called bile.

title: ANH15145 LDL and HDL Cholesterol
text_en: This video discusses two types of lipoproteins that carry cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein or LDL cholesterol, also known as bad cholesterol, and high-density lipoprotein or HDL cholesterol also known as good cholesterol.

title: ANH15145 LDL and HDL Cholesterol
text_en: LDL travels through your bloodstream, delivering cholesterol to the cells that need it. If your body has too much LDL, it can build up in the walls of your arteries. LDL and other substances in your artery wall form a fatty deposit called plaque.

title: ANH15145 LDL and HDL Cholesterol
text_en: Over time, plaque can narrow the artery and reduce blood flow. LDL carries cholesterol into the plaque. This is why LDL is called the bad cholesterol.

title: ANH15145 LDL and HDL Cholesterol
text_en: A commonplace this plaque can build up is in your coronary arteries, which are the blood vessels that feed your heart. This plaque build up causes coronary artery disease and increases your risk of a heart attack.

title: ANH15145 LDL and HDL Cholesterol
text_en: plaque build up in other arteries, such as the carotid arteries in your neck, can reduce blood flow to your brain and increase the risk of a stroke.

title: ANH15145 LDL and HDL Cholesterol
text_en: Your liver also makes high-density lipoprotein or HDL also known as the good cholesterol. HDL helps remove excess cholesterol from your cells, tissues, and from plaque in your blood vessels. This is why HDL is called the good cholesterol. HDL returns the excess cholesterol to your liver, which removes it from your body.

title: ANH15145 LDL and HDL Cholesterol
text_en: If after viewing this information, you have questions about cholesterol or any medications you've been prescribed to help manage your high cholesterol, speak with your healthcare provider. It is important to take your medications as directed by your provider and report any side effects you experience.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: If you or someone you know has been diagnosed with high cholesterol, it's important to keep it under control. This video demonstrates a few ways to help you manage your cholesterol, such as diet, exercise, and medication.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: If your cholesterol levels are too high, it's important to eat a heart-healthy diet. To eat a heart-healthy diet, you need to understand how the types of fats you eat can affect your cholesterol level.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: For example, saturated fats, hydrogenated fats, and trans fats tend to raise the bad cholesterol, also called low-density lipoprotein or LDL cholesterol in your blood. These fats are found in meat and dairy products, tropical oils, and many processed foods, such as cakes and cookies.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Facing saturated fats with unsaturated fats may help lower the bad cholesterol in your blood when eaten as part of a heart-healthy diet. Unsaturated fats include polyunsaturated and mono-unsaturated fats and are found in foods such as salmon, avocados, nuts, and olive oils.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Other heart-healthy lifestyle decisions you can make are to stop smoking and get adequate, moderate intensity aerobic exercise. Walking, biking, or swimming 30 minutes a day, five days a week, may be a good exercise

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Talk to your doctor about the type of diet and exercise plans most appropriate for your health and cholesterol levels.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: If lifestyle changes can't reduce your cholesterol levels enough, your doctor may prescribe certain medications to reduce it.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Modest medicines are the most common medication for lowering LDL or bad cholesterol by reducing the amount made in your liver. However, they have modest effects on lowering another type of fat in your blood called triglycerides, and raising good cholesterol, also called high-density lipoprotein or HDL cholesterol.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Your liver uses cholesterol to make a substance called bile, which helps digest your food. Cholesterol acid binding drugs bind to bile and prevent it from being used in digestion. This causes the liver to use more cholesterol to make more bile, thereby decreasing the amount of cholesterol in your bloodstream.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Cholesterol absorption inhibitors block some of the cholesterol taken up by your intestines from the food you eat.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Fibrates are mainly used to reduce triglyceride levels in your blood, but they can also raise HDL levels.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Cholesterol acid reduces LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and increases HDL cholesterol made in your liver.

title: ANH15146 High Cholesterol Management
text_en: Healthy lifestyle habits are important in controlling your cholesterol levels. If your healthcare provider prescribed medication to treat your high cholesterol, it is important to take it as directed and contact him or her if you have any questions.

title: ANH16169 Blood Test for Cholesterol
text_en: If you are watching this video, your healthcare provider has asked you or someone you know to get a blood test to measure your cholesterol levels. The National Institutes of Health cholesterol treatment guidelines recommend you have a blood test every five years if you are twenty years or older.

title: ANH16169 Blood Test for Cholesterol
text_en: The test, called a fasting lipoprotein profile, measures your levels of total cholesterol LDL, also known as bad cholesterol HDL, also known as good cholesterol and a type of fat in your blood called triglycerides.

title: ANH16169 Blood Test for Cholesterol
text_en: During this test, a blood sample will be taken after you have not eaten for nine to twelve Helping is important to ensure accurate results for this test.

title: ANH16169 Blood Test for Cholesterol
text_en: The total cholesterol goal should be less than 200mg per deciliter. For most people, an ideal HDL or good cholesterol should be 60 or higher. An ideal LDL or bad cholesterol should be less than 100. And fasting triglycerides should be less than 150.

title: ANH16169 Blood Test for Cholesterol
text_en: If you have high blood cholesterol levels, you have a greater risk of heart disease. In heart disease, blood vessels called coronary arteries become narrowed or blocked by a waxy substance containing cholesterol, called plaque. For time, plaque may reduce or block the flow of oxygen-rich blood to your heart muscle and cause a heart attack.
